items.title: Cardiovascular risk factors and CRVO
items.contentHtml:
Cardiovascular risk factors

  • High blood pressure (HBP) is the most common factor, present in >60% of cases.
  • Diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia and coronary artery disease increase the risk.
  • Blood hyperviscosity (polycythemia, macroglobulinemia) and thrombophilias (factor V Leiden mutation, antiphospholipid antibodies) are underlying causes in young patients.
  • Primary open-angle glaucoma is an independent risk factor for alteration of retinal venous flow.
  • Request complete blood count, lipid profile, HbA1c and blood pressure in all patients with CRVO.

items.title: Ischemic vs non-ischemic classification
items.contentHtml:
Ischemic vs non-ischemic classification

  • Non-ischemic CRVO (perfuse) represents 75% of cases; the ischemic CRVO (non-perfused) 25%.
  • Key criterion: area of capillary non-perfusion >10 areas of optic disc in fluorescein angiography defines the ischemic form.
  • The ischemic CRVO presents worse visual acuity (<20/200), pupillary defect. relative afferent (RPD) present and higher risk of neovascularization.
  • Non-ischemic CRVO has a better visual prognosis, absent DPAR and lower risk of complications.
  • Crucial early differentiation: ischemic requires panretinal laser photocoagulation to prevent neovascular glaucoma.

items.title: Unilateral painless visual loss
items.contentHtml:
Unilateral painless visual loss

  • Central retinal vein occlusion (CRVO) presents as unilateral painless visual loss sudden or progressive over hours.
  • Visual acuity varies from normal vision (mild forms) to counting fingers or hand movement in forms ischemic.
  • Main differential diagnosis: central retinal artery occlusion (more abrupt visual loss, cherry red spot without extensive hemorrhages).
  • Another cause of painless unilateral loss: anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION), which presents altitudinal field defect and disc edema.
  • Hemorrhage. Vitreous or retinal detachment also cause painless loss, but the fundus differentiates them.

items.title: Fundus: flame hemorrhages
items.contentHtml:
Fundo: flame hemorrhages

  • Classic finding: flame hemorrhages (superficial, in the nerve fiber layer) distributed in the four quadrants, often described as "snowstorm" or "bloody sunset".
  • There are also punctate hemorrhages or spotted (deeper layers) and cotton-wool spots (nerve fiber layer infarctions).
  • The retinal venules are dilated and tortuous, with frequent optic disc edema.
  • The presence of hard exudates suggests chronicity or an associated hypertensive component.
  • In the Non-ischemic CRVO hemorrhages may be less extensive and macular edema is the main cause of low vision.

items.title: Cherry red stain sign
items.contentHtml:
Cherry red spot sign

  • The cherry red spot is characteristic of central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO), not CRVO.
  • In CRVO, the fundus shows diffuse hemorrhages that mask the fovea, and the cherry red spot is not observed.
  • The spot Cherry red is produced by transparency of the choroid in the fovea, surrounded by whitish ischemic retina.
  • If a cherry red spot is seen in a CRVO, suspect combined arteriovenous occlusion (rare) or diagnostic error.
  • In the context of unilateral visual loss, the presence or absence of cherry red spot is key for the differential diagnosis.

items.title: Iris neovascularization
items.contentHtml:
Iris neovascularization

  • Iris neovascularization (NVI) is a late complication of ischemic CRVO, secondary to retinal ischemia that stimulates the release of VEGF. 100 days).
  • It is detected with slit lamp (gonioscopy) as thin, tortuous vessels on the surface of the iris and the chamber angle.
  • VIN can progress to neovascular glaucoma if untreated, due to peripheral angle closure by fibrovascular membranes.
  • Preventive treatment includes panretinal laser photocoagulation and intravitreal anti-VEGF for regression of NVI.

items.title: Secondary neovascular glaucoma
items.contentHtml:
Secondary neovascular glaucoma

  • Neovascular glaucoma secondary to ischemic CRVO is the most serious, with elevated intraocular pressure (IOP) >30 mmHg, pain and irreversible visual loss.
  • Mechanism: fibrovascular membrane in the chamber angle that closes the trabecular meshwork, preventing humor drainage
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with gonioscopy showing peripheral anterior synechiae and anomalous vessels.
  • First-line treatment: intravitreal anti-VEGF (for example bevacizumab 1.25 mg or ranibizumab 0.5 mg) to induce regression of neovascularization, followed by panretinal laser photocoagulation.
  • IOP control requires topical hypotensive agents (beta blockers, prostaglandin analogues) and, if refractory, glaucoma surgery (trabeculectomy with mitomycin C or Ahmed valve).

items.title: Diagnostic fluorescein angiography
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic fluorescein angiography

  • Fluorescein angiography (FGA) is the gold standard to classify CRVO into ischemic vs non-ischemic.
  • Diagnostic criteria: area of ​​capillary non-perfusion ≥10 optic disc areas indicates ischemic CRVO; <10 areas is non-ischemic.
  • Findings: delayed venous filling, capillary dilation, diffuse leaks due to macular edema and zones of ischemia that do not fluoresce.
  • AGF also detects early retinal neovascularization and edema. macular (leak in a petaloid pattern in the fovea).
  • Contraindications: allergy to fluorescein, renal failure (glomerular filtration <30 mL/min).

items.title: Intravitreal anti-VEGF treatment
items.contentHtml:
Intravitreal anti-VEGF treatment

  • Intravitreal anti-VEGF are the first-line treatment for macular edema secondary to CRVO.
  • Approved drugs: ranibizumab (0.5 mg/0.05 mL), aflibercept (2 mg/0.05 mL) and bevacizumab (1.25 mg/0.05 mL) used off-label but effective.
  • Typical regimen: monthly injections for 3 months (loading), then as needed (PRN) guided by OCT and visual acuity.
  • The anti-VEGF also reduce the risk of iris neovascularization and glaucoma neovascular in ischemic forms.
  • Adverse effects: endophthalmitis (0.02-0.05% per injection), subconjunctival hemorrhage, transient increase in IOP.

items.title: Panretinal laser photocoagulation
items.contentHtml:
Panretinal laser photocoagulation

  • Panretinal laser photocoagulation (PRP) is indicated in ischemic CRVO to prevent neovascularization of the iris and neovascular glaucoma.
  • Does not improve visual acuity; Its objective is to reduce the retinal ischemic load and the production of VEGF.
  • Technique: apply 1200-1600 burns of 200-500 µm in diameter in the peripheral retina, avoiding the macular area and the optic disc.
  • Specific indication: when there is neovascularization of the iris or angle, or in ischemic CRVO documented by angiography.
  • Adverse effects: peripheral visual field loss, nyctalopia, transient macular edema and choroidal effusion.

items.title: Control of elevated intraocular pressure
items.contentHtml:
Control of elevated intraocular pressure

  • In CRVO, intraocular pressure (IOP) may be elevated due to neovascular glaucoma or pre-existing primary open-angle glaucoma.
  • Acute control of IOP >30 mmHg requires topical hypotensive agents: timolol 0.5% (beta blocker) every 12 hours, brimonidine 0.2% (alpha-agonist) every 8 hours, and dorzolamide 2% (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor) every 8 hours.
  • If not controlled, add latanoprost 0.005% every 24 hours (analogue of prostaglandin) or oral acetazolamide 250 mg every 6-12 hours.
  • In neovascular glaucoma, intravitreal anti-VEGF reduce IOP by inducing regression of the fibrovascular membrane.
  • Surgery (trabeculectomy or Ahmed valve) is reserved for cases refractory to medical and laser treatment.

items.title: Association with hypertensive retinopathy
items.contentHtml:
Association with hypertensive retinopathy

  • Hypertensive retinopathy and CRVO share risk factors and fundus findings.
  • In chronic hypertensive retinopathy, arteriolar narrowing, pathological arteriovenous crossings (Gunn's sign), exudates are observed. hard spots and cotton-like spots.
  • Poorly controlled HTN predisposes to CRVO due to endothelial damage and increased venous resistance.
  • The presence of flame hemorrhages and disc edema in a CRVO may overlap with hypertensive changes; unilateral laterality suggests CRVO.
  • Always evaluate systemic blood pressure and fundus of the contralateral eye to rule out bilateral hypertensive retinopathy.
